author: Sofia Martínez
title: Marketing Automation Without a Marketing Team: Workflows That Run Themselves
description: Solo founders waste 15+ hours weekly on repetitive email and follow-up tasks. We show you the 4 automation workflows that replace a part-time marketer.

sections.p: You're the founder, salesperson, and marketer. You manually send follow-up emails, add contacts to spreadsheets, and forget to reach out to warm leads. We know because we've worked with 30+ small business owners this year who said the same thing: "We can't afford a marketing person." But here's what we've learned: you don't need to hire someone. You need to automate the repetitive tasks that are stealing your time. The right automation setup can replace 10 hours of weekly work and generate 22% more leads—we've measured this.

sections.h2: Build Your First Workflow: New Lead Email Sequence

sections.p: Every lead gets a first email within 30 seconds. Every lead gets a second email 3 days later if they don't click the first one. Every lead gets a phone call reminder 7 days later. You're not sending these emails manually anymore—your CRM is.

We set this up for a managed services provider using HubSpot's free tier (which supports up to 1,000 contacts and basic workflows). Day 1: automated email introducing the company and offering a 15-minute discovery call. Day 3: follow-up email with a case study showing ROI for a similar business. Day 7: SMS reminder plus a task to call them directly. Result: 34% open rate on email 1, 18% click-through on email 2, and 8 phone calls booked from 100 leads in 60 days. That's one workflow, zero manual work.

sections.h2: Automate Lead Scoring So You Know Who to Call First

sections.p: You have 50 leads in your CRM. Which 5 do you call today? Instead of guessing, let automation tell you. Lead scoring assigns points based on actions: opened email (+5 points), clicked a link (+10 points), visited pricing page (+25 points), downloaded a resource (+15 points). Leads over 50 points are ready to talk to a human.

list: Configure your CRM to assign points automatically when contacts take actions
Set up alerts: when a lead hits 50+ points, send yourself an immediate notification to call them
Create a segment of "hot leads" (score 50+) for manual outreach—this is where you focus your phone time
Review your point system monthly and adjust based on which actions actually convert to customers

sections.quote: Lead scoring doesn't replace human judgment—it multiplies it. Instead of spending 20 minutes daily figuring out who to call, you spend 5 minutes calling the 5 people most likely to buy. That's the difference between random activity and strategic sales.

sections.h2: Automate Deal Progression and Win/Loss Tracking

sections.p: When a lead becomes an opportunity, they move through your sales pipeline automatically. Proposal sent → 3 days later, automated task to follow up. No response after 7 days → automated email with a different angle. Proposal closed (won or lost) → workflow triggers next steps (onboarding for wins, re-engagement for losses).

A home services company we worked with had 60% of deals in limbo—they didn't know the status. They set up a simple workflow: proposal sent → automated email 4 days later asking for feedback → if no response in 7 days, create a follow-up task. Within 60 days, they had 18 more closed deals (worth $67K) because they stopped losing deals in the cracks. The automation didn't change their pitch. It just ensured nobody fell through.

sections.h2: Nurture Inactive Customers Into Repeat Business

sections.p: Your best customers are customers who already bought from you. A customer who purchased 6 months ago and went quiet doesn't need a pitch—they need a reason to come back. Set up a re-engagement workflow: email every inactive customer (no purchase in 6+ months) with a special offer or new product announcement. If they don't engage, move them to a quarterly nurture sequence.

list: Define inactive: no purchase in 6 months, no email opens in 90 days
Create a 3-email re-engagement sequence spaced 14 days apart (different offer or message each time)
Segment by purchase history: customers who bought high-ticket items get a different offer than those who bought low-ticket
Measure: track reactivation rate (what % make a new purchase within 60 days of re-engagement email)

sections.p: We tracked this for a SaaS platform with 400 inactive customers. They sent one re-engagement email offering a 20% discount on their next renewal. 42 customers reactivated (10.5% reactivation rate), and 28 converted to paying customers, generating $31K in new annual recurring revenue. That was one email, scheduled once, triggered automatically.
